cards.definition: a phospholipid bilayer controlling what enters and leaves the cell — described by the fluid mosaic model
the double layer of phospholipid molecules forming the cell membrane — hydrophilic heads face outward, hydrophobic tails face inward
the movement of water molecules through a partially permeable membrane from high water potential to low water potential
the movement of molecules against a concentration gradient using energy (ATP) — requires carrier proteins
the universal energy currency of cells — releases energy when broken down to ADP + phosphate
the process by which plants convert light energy into glucose using carbon dioxide and water — occurs in chloroplasts
the process by which cells release energy from glucose — aerobic uses oxygen; anaerobic does not
cell division producing two genetically identical cells — used for growth and repair
cell division producing four genetically unique cells with half the chromosome number — produces gametes
undifferentiated cells that can divide and specialise into different cell types — embryonic or adult
all the living organisms in an area plus their non-living environment, interacting together
a sequence showing the transfer of energy from one organism to the next through feeding
the position of an organism in a food chain — producers are level 1, primary consumers level 2, and so on
the total mass of living matter at each trophic level — biomass is lost at each level as energy is used for respiration
the process by which organisms with advantageous traits are more likely to survive and reproduce — driving evolution
the gradual change in inherited characteristics of a population over many generations
the formation of new species when populations become reproductively isolated and evolve separately
the maintenance of a stable internal environment despite changes in external conditions
a control mechanism where a change triggers a response that reverses the change — used in homeostasis e.g. temperature regulation
the variety of living organisms in an ecosystem — high biodiversity makes ecosystems more stable and resilient
